data.title: Constellation Software: Not Threatened By AI
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Giverny Capital Asset Management 2025 Annual Letter.

Constellation's (CNSWF) stock price has fallen by 26% in 2025 (and by another 12% to date in 2026). Such a decline is completely unrelated to the company's current situation. Indeed, revenue climbed 15% in 2025 and adjusted EPS by 21%. Since our initial purchase in 2014, EPS has grown at an annualized growth rate of 20%. Despite the recent stock price drop, the share price has still risen by more than 1000% since our first purchase 12 years ago.

In our opinion, Constellation Software is not as threatened by AI as some believe. And if it is in certain aspects of its business model, it can adapt (it already uses AI in several areas of its operations). It's important to understand that Constellation owns more than 1000 different companies acquired over the past 30 years, each occupying a niche in a specific market. Constellation has always prioritized software that drives businesses or key aspects of them, where seamless data continuity is essential, and where processes and user interfaces are paramount in their clients' daily operations. The software is often fully integrated into the client's operations while rarely representing a significant cost – relatively speaking. For example, it seems unrealistic to believe that an operator of a dozen mini-golf courses is going to start coding their own AI-powered reservation software to save something like $5,000 in annual fees!

The other news that affected – to a lesser extent – Constellation's stock is the departure of Mark Leonard. We have immense admiration for Mr. Leonard, and I often mentioned that he was my favorite CEO of the companies we own. Mark experienced serious health issues this fall, to the point where his life was in danger. He therefore had to resign and undergo emergency surgery. The good news is that the surgery went well, and Mark is now out of danger. He will therefore remain on the company's board of directors.

His replacement as CEO is Mark Miller, Mark Leonard's right-hand man since Constellation's inception in 1995. He was by his side throughout Constellation's development. In our opinion, there could be no better successor. Mark Miller has already addressed the entire company structure to better understand and adopt AI. To date, management has not seen any negative impact of AI on their operations, but they remain vigilant. Mark wants Constellation's various entities to be able to reinvent themselves if necessary to avoid becoming victims of AI. He has tasked all of Constellation's diverse units trying to solve new problems for their clients using AI and generating new revenue streams through it. AI could even be an indirect opportunity for Constellation: the company grows primarily through acquisitions, and clearly, the cost of potential target companies has just dropped dramatically. A lower price paid means a better return on invested capital.

We had already reduced our stake in Constellation several times (when it exceeded 10% of the portfolio). We had also sold our shares in Topicus and Lumine – shares distributed at zero cost by Constellation – because they seemed to be trading at high levels. Both stocks have also fallen by about 50% since then. We therefore managed the risk appropriately. Nevertheless, the decline in Constellation's share price hurt our portfolio in 2025 and again in early 2026. We estimate the negative impact of the share price decline for 2025 at 2%. We have confidence in both Constellation's resilient business model and the qualities of its new CEO. We therefore intend to keep our shares while, of course, closely monitoring the situation.

Forward-looking information

Some information set forth in this letter constitutes forward-looking information which involves uncertainties and other known and unknown factors that may cause actual results or events to differ materially from those anticipated in such forward-looking information. When used in this letter, words such as "expects", "anticipates", "intends", "may", "believes" and similar expressions generally identify forward-looking information. In developing the forward-looking information contained in this letter, the manager has made assumptions (for ex.: with respect to the outlook for the global economy and publicly traded companies). These assumptions are based on the manager's perception of factors believed to be relevant (for ex.: historical trends, current conditions, expected future developments). Although the manager believes that the assumptions made and the expectations represented by such information are reasonable, there can be no assurance that the forward-looking information will prove to be accurate. Actual results or events may differ materially from those expressed or implied in the forward-looking information. Giverny Capital Inc. undertakes no obligation to publicly update or revise these forward-looking statements.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hillman Solutions: Market Share Gains And Pricing Power To Navigate Uncertainty
data.description:
Synopsis

Hillman Solutions Corp. (HLMN) is a provider of a wide range of products, including fasteners, ropes, chains, personal protective equipment, and key duplication systems. The company provides the products to retail markets such as hardware stores, home centres, and mass merchants. The major customers of the company are Home Depot and Lowe’s.

In July last year, I recommended a Hold rating on the stock due to high exposure to Chinese imports, which were subject to increased tariffs. However, the company has decreased the exposure of suppliers based in China from 49% to 32%. Furthermore, HLMN plans to limit this exposure to 20%. This shift towards a diversified supply chain strengthens the investment case of the stock.

HLMN’s plan to win new business and increase its market share should help to navigate the current macroeconomic uncertainty. With the price increase, the company is expected to offset the headwind on revenue from decreased market volume in FY26. On the profitability front, the margins are expected to face tough comparison in the coming quarters.

Revenue Analysis & Outlook

In FY2025, the net sales of the company were $1.552 billion, an increase of 5.4% year-over-year. The improvement was driven by the 3% (y-o-y) benefits from the acquisition of Intex DIY and 2% benefits from the new business wins. Moreover, the price increase also contributed 5.5% (y-o-y) to the net sales. However, the market volume negatively impacted the revenue, down 5% year-over-year.

Looking ahead, the company should witness increased revenue year-over-year in FY2026. The management has given net sales guidance from $1.6 to $1.7 billion, with a midpoint estimate of $1.65 billion. The growth in net sales should be supported by new business wins and price increases. However, the market volume should be a headwind to the revenue of the company. Apart from organic growth, the company should continue to pursue acquisitions in the future.

S&P Global US Manufacturing PMI was at 52.4, rising from February's PMI of 51.6, implying growth in the manufacturing activity. Production growth increased in March as new orders rose to the highest level since last October. Moreover, the rise in raw material inventory also contributed to the expansion of PMI as the factories restocked to avoid war-related shipping issues and maintain timely delivery.

March PMI was above the 50.0 threshold, indicating expansion in the manufacturing activity. Despite the positive momentum, some benefits could be related to the precautionary inventory build amid the supply chain disruption from the war. Furthermore, the management of HLMN indicates that the market volume should be between down and flat on a year-over-year comparison.

Hillman’s competitive differentiator consists of a 1,200-member field sales and service team. This enables the company to directly ship to the customer’s retail location rather than their distribution centres. The company, by leveraging the competitive moat with its retail partner, should continue to increase its market share regardless of the macroeconomic conditions.

Throughout the quarters of FY2025, the company increases the price to mitigate the tariff impacts on the input costs. For instance, in the third quarter of FY2025, the pricing contributed approximately 10 points to the increase in net sales. The carryover impact of these pricing actions from previous quarters is expected to provide support to the revenue of HLMN. Overall, the management expects mid-single-digit (y-o-y) benefits on revenue from prior pricing action.

The company should continue to pursue several bolt-on acquisition opportunities in the future. At the end of FY2025, the net debt outstanding was around $665.8 million, an improvement of $8 million from the previous year. Currently, the net debt to adjusted EBITDA (TTM) ratio stood at 2.4x, an improvement from 2.8x in FY24. Therefore, the strong financial position should help the company to execute its M&A strategy.

Margin Analysis & Outlook

In FY2025, the adjusted gross margin of the company stood at 48.7%, an improvement of 60 basis points on a year-over-year comparison. Moreover, the adjusted EBITDA margin was at 17.7%, an increase of 130 basis points. The growth in margins was driven by a favourable price/cost spread in FY2025. Moreover, the operating leverage also contributed to margin expansion as selling, general, and administrative expenses as a % of sales declined in FY2025.

Looking ahead, I believe the margins are expected to face tough comparison from FY2025 and normalize throughout FY2026. The margins are expected to face pressure due to the timing of price increases and higher tariff costs flowing through the income statement.

In the second half of FY2025, the company implemented the price increases to mitigate the higher tariff-related costs. At the same time, the company had lower-cost inventory, related to pre-tariff times, flowing through the income statement. This drives the margins to the peak levels in the third quarter of FY25; however, the margins began to stabilize in Q4.

While the pre-tariff costs were in FY25, the higher input costs are expected to be fully realised in FY26. Therefore, the benefit from a favourable price/cost spread on margins is expected to diminish. Accordingly, the management expects the adjusted gross margin to be down on a year-over-year basis, ranging between 46% & 47%.

Valuation

Hillman Solutions Corp. is currently trading at a non-GAAP P/E (fwd) of 14.15x with a consensus EPS estimate of $0.58. The company is expected to increase its market share through its competitive advantage. This, coupled with price increases, should help the company to offset the headwinds from the decline in market volume. Moreover, the margins are expected to face pressure from the previous year's strong results. Conclusively, I believe the company should achieve the consensus estimate for FY2026.

Furthermore, I have used relative valuation to compare the company with its peers based on the revenue growth rate, operating margin, and P/E ratio. Looking at the revenue growth rate, the company is expected to drive 5.62% year-over-year, which is higher than the peer forward revenue growth rate of 3.22%. Moreover, the EBIT margin of the company is at 7.45%, significantly lower than the peer median margin of 18.97%. When comparing HLMN’s P/E ratio of 14.15x with its peers, it is lower than the median P/E ratio.

Risk

HLMN's sales are dependent on the stability and growth of the economy. Any adverse effects on the economic factors, especially the industrial sector, may impact the financial performance of the company. Additionally, if the company isn't able to increase its market share, it could limit revenue growth in the coming quarters.

Conclusion

With a peer group P/E ratio of 18.9x, the company is trading at a discount of approximately 25%. The peer group median, according to my relative model, is slightly different from the sector median on Seeking Alpha, as I have used peers specific to the industry of the company. HLMN is well-positioned to navigate the macroeconomic uncertainty through business wins and price. Moreover, the company is diversifying its supply chain, particularly by reducing Chinese imports, which enhances earnings visibility. Considering these factors, there should be a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: General Dynamics: Poised To Benefit From High Volume Of Government Contracts
data.description:
The war in Ukraine and recent military action in the Middle East reaffirm that military conflicts are not a thing of the past. The upper hand in any conflict is often determined by the arsenal an army uses. A more modern arsenal decreases the number of casualties and increases not only the raw power in a physical conflict but also a nation's military reputation. As a result of constant innovation, contracts for better equipment keep coming in.

The US is the world's leading military power, representing 37% to 40% of total global military spending in 2025. For General Dynamics (GD), this means that as long as there is reason for the US to remain as the world's greatest power, money from the government will keep coming in. Conflict remains a recurring element of international relations, and since World War 2, the United States has maintained an active presence in major military operations. More than any other nation. In spite of competition, General Dynamics is one of the United States' highest-priced army contractors, and record-breaking contracts keep coming in. Advancements in AI and weaponry all around the world require that every nation (such as the US) keep spending on newer and better equipment. This significantly benefits General Dynamics. They are therefore rated as a Buy, but I do recommend waiting for a price drop.

Financial Highlights

General Dynamics closed the 2025 fiscal year with significant top- and bottom-line expansion, showing resilience to things like material inflation, skilled labor shortages, or supply chain interruptions. For the full year, General Dynamics reported a top line of 52.6 billion, a 10% year-over-year increase. Earnings per share increased by 13.25% to $15.6.

An indicator of the company's robust health is their strong cash-generating capability. The five-year free cash flow average is $3.56 billion compared to a net income of $3.52 billion. For the full year, operating cash flow reached $5.1 billion, 122% of net earnings. General Dynamics directs a large proportion of their cash into capital expenditures. In 2025, they invested $1.2 billion, which represents a 27% increase compared to 2024. Most of these expenses go towards expanding the Marine Systems and Aerospace segments. The majority of their free cash flow goes towards dividend payments (10-year CAGR 8.2%, 5-year CAGR 6.5%). Yearly dividend increases are decreasing, but this is done to keep the payout ratio in perfect health at around 40%. Even with heavy capital expenditures in 2025, the company still managed to close the year with a $2.3 billion cash position, more than 80% of their average yearly free cash flow, demonstrating the company's health.

Near Future

The most crucial indicator for the near future of General Dynamics is their order backlog. Driven by high demand, it experienced exceptional growth. By the end of 2025, the backlog was valued at a staggering $118 billion, a year-over-year increase of 30%. This suggests that the elevated revenue growth the company experienced in 2024 and 2025 is nowhere near over. For 2026, management forecasts revenue to range between $54.4 and $54.8 billion. This means an increase of 4.3% year over year. Earnings per share are expected to grow at a 5-year CAGR of 6.9%, 1.5% faster than across the latest decade, reaching a value of between $16.1 and $16.2 for the year. In the near term, if backlog is converted to revenue as planned, financial analysts expect EPS to increase by 7.37% in 2026 and by 10.46% by 2027, growing fastest during the first half of the next 5 years, opening an opportunity for a short-term position.

President Donald Trump also proposed in early January a $1.5 trillion defense budget for 2027. This represents a 50% increase from the current budget of around $900 billion. Although I believe this budget proposal is unlikely to go through at its full capacity, it certainly highlights the bullish sentiment surrounding military spending and army contractor growth in the coming years.

Growth Drivers

The largest growth driver over the past several years has been the General Dynamics Electric Boat, which represents their Marine systems segment. This segment is by far the company's largest, accounting for 31.8% of total revenue in 2025. (Technologies 25.6%, Combat Systems 17.6%, Aerospace 24.9%). The Marine systems segment is also growing the fastest, at a rate of 8.2% per year over the last decade. In Q4 of 2025, it increased by 22% year-over-year to $4.8 billion, accompanied by a 73% surge in operating earnings.

This segment is General Dynamics most prized asset. Currently, they are working on the Columbia-class submarine, which will be the largest submarine the US has ever constructed, 2.5 times bigger than the current Virginia-class. Electric Boat is responsible for approximately 78% of the project, and in March, it was awarded an additional $15.38 billion for a contract modification, on top of the $25 billion in contracts for this specific vessel. The reason why these vessels are the US's most prized possessions is because they carry around 70% of the available nuclear arsenal, which is still the deadliest force in the world. I believe that due to ongoing and future conflicts, nuclear warhead carriers will always be the priority for the US. They will not hesitate to spend billions of dollars on the newest equipment, which will likely go to contracts with General Dynamics, as they are the go-to contractor for marine weaponry.

The marine systems segment isn't the only growing segment. Within the past five years, the Combat systems segment, responsible for the infamous M2 Bradley, has been growing at a compounded annual growth rate of 5.7%. The Aerospace segment has also experienced a growth spurt across the last 2 years. It grew from $8.6 billion in 2023 to $13.1 billion in 2025 (an increase of 52%). From 2016 to 2023, it has been stagnant at around $8 billion. The reason for this one-time jump in revenue was not due to army contracts, but instead the fulfillment of the Gulfstream backlog. Following the Boeing 737 MAX issues, the FAA required Gulfstream to perform time-consuming inspections, which delayed the delivery of already built jets to customers. After the inspections were successfully completed, the full backlog was released in the two following years. This resulted in a one-time growth spurt.

The global business jet market, currently valued at approximately $20.6 billion, is projected to reach $28.5 billion by 2034 (CAGR 3.5%). Gulfstream alone expects to expand from $3.5 billion in 2026 to $4.4 billion by 2031. This means the 50% jump in revenue will not be a norm for the coming years. I expect the Aerospace segment to return to their sluggish pre-2023 revenue growth.

In spite of long-term sluggish Aerospace growth, General Dynamics has moved up in the ranks of the biggest army contractors for the US. In 2016, they were the 4th biggest contractor after Lockheed Martin, Boeing, and Raytheon (RTX). In 2023, they moved to the third position right after RTX. Within these past 9 years (since 2016), the value of yearly contracts has increased from $11.8 billion to approximately $23.43 billion in fiscal year 2025. It is visible that the US has been increasingly spending more money on military contractors within the last decade.

Considering General Dynamics' growth drivers, I remain very bullish. The US shows no signs of stopping investing trillions of dollars into defense and weaponry, which is by far the biggest growth driver for army contractors. General Dynamics is also showing exceptional resilience to the macroeconomic environment. Considering delays in their Gulfstream subsection, they still manage to fulfill their full backlog. The current backlog also leads me to believe that their topline will keep growing in the coming years. High cash reserves and low payout ratios additionally highlight their health and stability.

Valuation and Peer Comparison

Deriving from the historical growth of financials, analyst predictions, and General Dynamics healthy growth drivers, we will predict a few possible scenarios for future growth and fair share value using the standard DCF model. Historically, the free cash flow has been growing as such: 5-year CAGR. 6.5%, 10-year CAGR. 7.45%. If the current backlog is fulfilled, the US increases their army spending, and General Dynamics keeps their position as the third largest army contractor in the US, the future CAGR could reach a value of 7-8%. The discount rate for our calculation is 8%, which factors in market risk. From these inputs we get a fair value of 335 (3.2% lower than the current $346 per share)

Investing in General Dynamics carries risks. The marine system is facing a lack of physical capacity (skilled workers). In early February 2026, it was disclosed that the subsidiary plans to hire 8,000 new workers by the end of the year. This is a huge jump compared to the personnel hired in 2025 (3,000). Given the current amount of employees (25,000), this will be a 25% jump. It is possible that Electric Boat will not be able to find this many workers in such a short period of time, which might slow down growth of their leading segment. There is also risk from competitors. In the Aerospace segment, Gulfstream must protect its market share from aggressive positioning from Bombardier. Also, the private jet market is reliant on a very specific market that is easily shaken by severe macroeconomic contraction. Analysts furthermore expect a shortage of critical aircraft parts as a near-term risk. If it came to these scenarios, I predict that the free cash flow CAGR would come in between 5 and 7%. With a discount rate of 8%, the fair value is $295, 15% lower than the current price.

The company's PEG ratio (price to earnings to growth) is sitting rather high (3.27). Peer companies (Lockheed Martin, Parker-Hannifin, and Howmet Aerospace), however, have a very similar ratio, between 2.68 and 3.81. When measured against its peers, General Dynamics appears fairly priced. Although analysts expect a price of around $400 in 6-12 months, I believe that within this time frame, a better buying opportunity will come when General Dynamics is trading at a fairer price.

To change my long-term bullish sentiment, General Dynamics would have to visibly start losing contracts from the US to its competitors. Otherwise, all other risks, like skilled labor shortages or supply chain disruptions, are unlikely to shake the company long term. The biggest risk is competition. Even though General Dynamics has been holding one of the leading positions as an army contractor for the US and is slowly climbing upwards, there is never enough cautiousness, especially because the company is heavily reliant on US contracts (40-50% of revenue). Losing them to competitors, although unlikely, would make me quickly re-evaluate my Buy rating.

Final Recommendation

Overall, General Dynamics is at this time slightly overvalued but is very healthy. Their four segments make them very resilient to various micro- and macroeconomic downturns. They also own Electric Boat, which is the US's go-to contractor for marine defense, including nuclear defense, which is still the most feared weapon set in the world. Conflicts in the Middle East, Ukraine, and elsewhere force the US to increasingly spend more money on military contracts, which plays into General Dynamics cards well. I rate the stock as a long-term Buy, but recommend waiting for a price drop or the market's bearish overreaction to invest.

data.title: Trilogy Metals: A Speculative Medium-Risk/High-Reward Play That Is A Proxy Bet On Executive Power
data.description:
Trilogy Metals Inc. (TMQ) is a pure play on the development of a major new Alaskan mining district, the Upper Kobuk District, with two projects—Arctic and Bornite—currently in the advanced exploration/early permitting stage. While the project faces inherent risks and a turbulent past related to Arctic logistics and environmental permitting, it has an experienced player (South32) as a JV partner, a uniquely very high-quality copper asset, and is backed by the Department of Defense (DoD), which has taken a direct 10% stake for its strategically important byproducts like cobalt, germanium, and gallium.

As it stands, this is a speculative medium-risk/high-reward regulatory bet on the DoD's answer to managing permitting and pushing through environmental roadblocks.

Valuing the asset

The Arctic project finished its feasibility study in 2023. We can use those numbers there and scale them a bit to look at the present NPV of the project. Pre-tax NPV was USD 1.5 billion; after-tax NPV was USD 1.1 billion. This is a tax discount rate of 26.67%, but that was in 2023. Since then, the phasing down of bonus depreciation, Section 45X credit, and Alaskan corporate tax sunset plans make this discount much lighter, at around 18%.

Meanwhile, we need to scale up the pre-tax NPV from 2023, considering the massive increase in asset prices since then. This is tricky because of the mixed poly-metallic nature of the asset (see the feasibility study for the numbers), but prices in precious heavy metals (Au/Ag) have scaled up roughly 2.5x, while copper and zinc have seen modest gains (40% and 16%, respectively), and lead has seen a pullback. We also need to consider that mining costs have increased by roughly 20%, while at the same time margins have expanded massively for copper mines with silver byproduct. Considering all of this, the pre-tax NPV scales up roughly by 2x to USD 3 billion. Discounting this by 18%, we get a back-of-the-envelope post-tax NPV of USD 2.46 billion.

Meanwhile, a 2025 preliminary economic assessment for the Bornite project pegs its after-tax NPV at just under USD 400 million. Since it's a 2025 study, we are better off not trying to scale this one. So now combined, the NAV of the company's two assets comes up to roughly USD 3 billion as a ballpark figure. TMQ currently has a market cap of USD 580 million. This is a P/NAV of just under 0.2x.

If you want to be conservative about the scaling factor used in the previous paragraph, you'd still get a post-tax NPV of around USD 1.8-2 billion for the Arctic project, which translates to a P/NAV of 0.25-0.3x in the most conservative scenarios. On the face of it, this still looks very good for a junior developer backed by the US DoD, whose involvement significantly de-risks the project and makes the economics look very attractive. By comparison, Solaris Resources Inc. (SLS) or Arizona Sonoran Copper Company Inc. (ASCUF) are trading around 0.4x despite being in younger stages.

The main reason for the discount and the opportunity is the regulatory risk discussed below.

The Legal Risk and Discount

TMQ's fate hinges on the Ambler Access Project—a 211-mile industrial access road that is the lifeline of the mine. The permits for this road have been subject to political football between the Trump and Biden administrations. While Trump 1.0 granted the permits, they were effectively suspended by the Department of Interior (DoI) under the Biden administration. Since then, the second Trump administration has aggressively pursued Alaska's resource potential, and in June 2025, TMQ filed an extraordinary appeal under Section 1106 of the Alaskan National Interest Lands Conservation Act (ANILCA), leading to what is effectively an executive order restoring the old 2020 approval, as well as a presidential grant of appeal under Section 1106, following which the DoI reissued the 50-year right-of-way permit within two weeks. A number of risks remain, however—some on paper, some in real life.

Despite the President's order reinstating the permits and the appeal under S. 1106, a lawsuit cycle is inevitable, and already a coalition of environmental groups has filed one in Anchorage. The road also crosses lands owned by Alaska Native Corporations (NANA), and though there is talk of an MoU, a final agreement is still pending. The reversal of approvals and the no-use stand of the DoI under President Biden had sent the stock crashing 60%. It has since recovered and made new peaks post the renewed approvals, but the shock and risk factor linger.

In my subjective opinion as a lawyer, the S. 1106 appeal grant requires Congressional approval, but the order to reinstate the previously granted approval does not and remains solid. The real risk in more business-as-usual times would come from administrative infighting. The present executive, however, has been unprecedented in its pursuit of resource dominance, and it's hard to imagine the DoI directly taking on the DOD, which is now an investor in the project.

The Arctic project is singular in its high-quality copper ore, and with the US actively pursuing military and resource dominance, the idea of environmental concerns being able to stall the DoD seems less likely today than it has been in the past (though “you never know” always applies). The permitting process should also be helped by the FAST-41 designation, which provides a dashboard with strict timelines for every single agency permit. There is a direct correlation here with the President's own political power—any significant weakening of the executive, especially after the midterms, would give a second wind to department bureaucrats and alter the regulatory risk-reward ratio dramatically.

I would be less concerned about the litigation challenge—as long as the administration remains united, the most likely outcome is a settlement, at most with some delays. But I would definitely like to see some gravel on the ground and the FAST-41 dashboard in place before the midterms. AIDEA, the Alaskan authority that will build the road, is currently targeting a May/June 2026 start. If these targets are met and a start has been made by late 2026, which is what the plan seems to be, it would significantly reduce any remaining risk to the project from a negative outcome in the midterms. Once the road issue gets settled, the actual permitting for the mine remains, but at that point the valuation should move up towards 0.4-0.5x P/NAV, which is near 2x upside. The DoD could then exercise its warrants to take the total ownership up to 17.5%. It would also unlock the potential for other discoveries in the district, so at that point not only will the P/NAV inch up, but the NAV itself could look very different.

As a footnote, investor John Paulson has divested most of his 9% stake in Trilogy post the price surge after Ambler Road was approved. However, as noted here, this could just as well be profit-taking and streamlining to focus on his Donlin project, with other investors staying put, including Alaskan Native leader Willie Hensley and billionaire Thomas Kaplan.

Conclusion

As an investment, value unlock in TMQ, for the immediate future, is basically a bet on the US federal government's executive power and its willingness to use it. There is a terrific asset underneath, which partially limits the downside to this bet—even with significant delays, the nature of the asset doesn't change, and TMQ's USD 51 million in free cash, with the first leg of investment in Arctic being funded by the DoD, gives it enough buffer to absorb a few delays and losses. Meanwhile, progress on the road construction in 2026 is positioned to unleash at least a 50% upside in 2026 and clear the way for this to grow from a depressed junior miner stock towards a 0.5x type P/NAV as permitting falls into place.

The entire value in this bet comes from the risk discount associated with the access road and its traumatic permitting history. Ultimately, the perception of that risk depends on your perception of the executive's ability to get things done. For me, it's the nature of this administration and its loud and clear priorities, backed by executive power, that make the regulatory risk-reward seem like a source of value. As with many things associated with this administration, this is a big, chaotic bet. That's not a bad thing. Every investor will have their own opinion on these variables, but if you are interested in putting some money aside for a high-risk, high-reward bet, then you might like this one.

data.title: Copper Miners Face Energy Delivery, Supply Chain Uncertainty
data.description:
Knock-on effects from the conflict in Iran and subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz are expected to have a delayed but meaningful impact on the primary non-ferrous markets, especially through upstream supply chains.

Building on our recent scenario analysis for iron ore, we adjusted our base case for 2026 to incorporate changes in key cost metrics affecting mining operations.

Factors such as rising fuel and reagent prices due to ongoing geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East are expected to significantly impact the output and profitability of copper mines. Our modeling projects a 5.1% cost increase across the global copper mining industry, with reagent prices playing a crucial role in this escalation.

Sulfuric acid, sulfur costs rise on an already tightened supply

Sulfuric acid is essential in the copper mining industry, serving multiple functions, including acting as a leaching agent to extract copper from oxide ores and as a pH modifier during processing.

A byproduct of the oxidation process, sulfur dioxide gas, can be captured and converted back into sulfuric acid, enhancing operational efficiency. Typically, sulfuric acid concentrations hover around 95%. However, operations lacking sufficient in-house production of this reagent may face challenges due to supply chain disruptions stemming from the ongoing conflict.

With a significant portion of sulfur sourced from the Middle East, prices for both sulfuric acid and solid sulfur have risen amid uncertainty regarding trade flows since the start of the disruption.

The Platts-assessed Solid Sulfur FOB Middle East Spot (excl. Iran) surged to almost $700 per metric ton at close on March 12, a weekly gain of over 34%, while the Platts-assessed Sulfuric Acid CFR Indonesia shifted higher to $170/mt at close on March 11. Given the sensitivity of the sulfuric acid supply chain to geopolitical events, market participants are closely monitoring the situation.

The power problem: impacts on mining operations

The closure of the strait has stifled shipping of vital oil and LNG supplies, affecting primary production due to storage capacities nearing their limits. Additionally, with refineries such as Saudi Arabian Oil Co.'s Ras Tanura and Bapco Energies Sitra sites both being affected by missile and drone strikes, pipeline deliveries are projected to be insufficient to meet demand.

Fuel and electricity costs are rising rapidly, and the prolonged nature of these disruptions will undoubtedly affect copper mining operations. Many sites run their light and heavy machinery on diesel fuel, and mills are generally reliant on grid power to process ore.

Processes like semi-autogenous grinding (SAG) milling, for example, require large amounts of energy, contributing to the industry's consumption of approximately 1.7% of global energy generated. With some grids in European and Asian markets reliant on oil or LNG as their fuel sources, miners in those regions are highly exposed to the ongoing disruption in supply.

The ramifications for African operations may be even greater and possibly exceed our initial modeling assumptions. While Nigeria and Libya are significant oil exporters, providing an opportunity for increased intra-African trade, the continent remains heavily reliant on fuel imports from the Middle East.

Many regions in Africa depend on oil-based fuels for power generation and utilize diesel generators as a backup to mitigate grid instability. In contrast, countries with substantial renewable energy infrastructure - such as Canada, where over 60% of the grid is powered by hydropower - appear relatively insulated from the geopolitical tensions affecting other regions.

Modeling a cost impact scenario: assumptions and inputs

To evaluate the near-term impacts against our 2026 consensus forecast base case, we have incorporated several key assumptions: a variable increase in electricity costs (up to 40%, depending on the region), a 25% year-over-year rise in fuel costs for 2026, a 40% increase in reagent costs, and a 15% hike in transportation rates.

Our updated scenario reflects these theoretical rate hikes across multiple cost metrics, indicating a cost increase of over 10 cents per pound for paid copper as a direct consequence of the ongoing conflict. The most significant impact for copper miners will be on the operational side, where mining and processing costs will account for 84% of the total increase.

Reagent costs are projected to rise the most, increasing 36% compared to our 2026 base case forecast, translating to nearly 7 cents/lb when compared to the 2025 consensus scenario. Fuel costs also pose a significant risk to cash margins, contributing to a 2 cents/lb increase from 2025 rates and an 18% change compared to our original 2026 forecast.

The uncertainty surrounding the duration of the crisis will likely tighten profit margins for operators and expose vulnerabilities in both upstream and downstream supply chains. With diminishing market sentiment, suppliers to mining operations may be compelled to declare force majeure if they cannot fulfill contractual obligations, which could, in the long term, lead to declines in primary copper production.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings: Downgrade To Hold For The Near Term
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings (BJ) was a buy rating because the visibility to 10% EPS growth over the coming years got a lot clearer. As much as I like the long-term setup, I am downgrading to hold because FY2026 now looks more investment-heavy than earnings-heavy, and that MFI growth is normalizing.

The Long-Term Story Looks Better

I would say that BJ's core business looks stronger than it did in my previous write-up. The main positive development is membership quality. BJ is not just adding more members. It is adding better members. The base is larger, renewal rates are holding up well at a high level, and it has penetrated deeper into the higher-tier cohorts. Those members also account for a bigger share of merchandise spend. The other important development is that new club growth has shown up in the numbers. BJ opened 14 clubs in FY2025, and management said this cohort of new clubs is performing above expectations on all major KPIs: sales, membership, and profit. Clearly, this shows that new boxes are working early and are working better than expected. This really adds weight to the long-term unit growth story.

The digital side of things is also bullish. In my previous write-up, I focused mainly on reduced delivery fees and the idea that BJ was making online ordering easier. I still think that logic makes sense, but BJ's recent results got me even more bullish on this. It seems like more members are choosing to shop through BJ's digital channels, and if members use BJ more often for pickup, delivery, or online orders, BJ can capture an even bigger share of their weekly spending. In other words, digital is not just making shopping more convenient; it is actually a way to get members to spend more with BJ.

The Next Leg Looks More Investment-Heavy Than Earnings-Heavy

However, I think the current equity story has pivoted away from focusing on the 10% adj. EPS growth story to a "weaker EPS growth" story in the near term. The problem is not that anything structural has broken. The problem is headline growth normalization and the step up on the cost side.

For the former, it relates to membership fee income [MFI]. It was strong in Q4 2025, but headline growth is likely going to slow as BJ laps the January 2025 fee increase, and growth would shift back toward a more normal run rate. To be clear, this doesn't mean the health of the membership base has deteriorated. As I said above, the long-term outlook remains healthy. Renewal remains strong, and the mix is still improving. But it does change the earnings setup. MFI has been a strong contributor to earnings growth (high incremental margin, and it's easy to forecast). Now that MFI growth is going to normalize in FY2026, the headline contribution is going to be less prominent in the near term.

For the latter, it is going to weigh on near-term EPS growth. Q4 2025 already showed some pressure. Merchandise gross margin ex-gas and ex-membership fee income was down, and SG&A as a percentage of sales went up. Looking into FY2026, the setup does not look very earnings-friendly. BJ's guidance is for 2% to 3% comp growth and $4.40 to $4.60 of adj. EPS (~3% y/y growth at the midpoint). This suggests that topline growth is not dropping through cleanly to earnings. That makes sense given the heavier investment backdrop, which management has indicated a willingness to invest in price, club openings, and stepping up digital capabilities. The way I see it, BJ is choosing to protect growth and strengthen the business over the long term, which is strategically right, but the near-term trade-off is weaker earnings growth.

Valuation

I still like the long-term outlook, wherein the improving membership quality and new club expansion remain strong. However, the reason for downgrading to hold is because the near-term setup is not as clean as I like it to be. I don't doubt the 10% long-term EPS growth outlook, but in the near term, we could see EPS grow just low-to-mid-single-digits (because of SG&A creeping up and MFI growth normalizing). The market is unlikely to re-rate BJ's multiple upwards against this EPS growth backdrop. If we comp against other players that have low-to-mid-single-digit EPS growth, like Dollar Tree (DLTR), one could even argue that BJ trades at an expensive level (DLTR trades at 15x while BJ trades at 21.5x NTM PE). The pushback is that BJ has solid potential to re-accelerate to 10% EPS growth again in FY2027 and beyond, and for that reason, I think the market is not completely downgrading BJ to DLTR's multiple. All in all, I think it's fair that the market pushed down multiple in the near term.

Besides, I always fall back to what's the best alternative to invest in given valuation and earnings growth. If I want to invest in BJ today at 21x NTM PE with 3% adj. EPS growth—why not invest in the S&P index, which trades at ~19.5x NTM PE and has mid-teens adj. EPS growth?

Conclusion

I still like BJ's long-term setup. Membership quality is improving, new clubs are ramping up better than expected, and digital is giving members more ways to spend. But the near-term setup is less clean than before. Near-term adj. EPS growth is going to moderate as MFI growth normalizes and management leans into price, club openings, and digital investment. For this reason, my view on BJ is a hold rating for now.

data.title: Chinese PMI Rebounds As Iran Impacts Start Trickling Through
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

Manufacturing PMI returns to expansion despite Iran War

China's manufacturing PMI rebounded to 50.4 in March, up sharply from 49.0 in February. This came in stronger than market expectations and reached a 12-month high. Although the official PMI and industrial production trends have generally shown a looser-than-normal correlation over the past year, they nonetheless suggest a solid start to the year for China's industrial activity.

The recovery was broad-based. Production (51.4), new orders (51.6), new export orders (49.1), and employment (48.6) all increased on the month. The new export orders subindex, in particular, reached its highest level since April 2024, as external demand continues to buoy growth in China.

We’re starting to see some of the impact of the Iran war and higher energy prices in the subindices. First, the purchase price of raw materials subindex surged to 63.9, up from 54.8. This is the highest monthly read since April 2022.

Second, ex-factory prices also rose to 55.4, up from 50.6, hitting a 48-month high. This suggests that inflationary pressure is set to pick up in the months ahead. Persistently higher prices could begin to weigh more substantively on activity in the coming months.

Price subindices starting to show impact from Iran war

Non-manufacturing PMI also returned to expansion

China's non-manufacturing PMI also beat expectations, rebounding to 50.1 in March. This snapped a two-month contraction streak. The uptick appears to be primarily due to a smaller contraction in new export orders, which rose to a 6-month high of 48.8 from 44.7.

We also saw upticks in input prices in the non-manufacturing PMI index. This is another data point supporting China's reflationary theme. Generally, the other subcategories remained quite soft, indicating that the services sector remains under pressure.

Business expectations, which have consistently been an outperformer in the non-manufacturing PMI subindices, fell to 54.2, the lowest level since 2022. Overall, PMI data has held up quite well in the first full month since the Iran war broke out.

China remains relatively well-positioned to endure short-term disruptions. But if higher energy prices and shipping disruptions persist or worsen, we could see pressure build in the months ahead.

Non-manufacturing PMI barely returns to expansion but subindices still look weak

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: South Korean Data Points To Firm First Quarter Growth, But Iran Risks Abound
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Semiconductor output rose firmly thanks to high-end memory chip production

Korea’s industrial production rebounded more than expected by 5.4% month-on-month, seasonally adjusted, in February (vs. revised -2.4% in January, 1.5% market consensus).

The output rebound was concentrated in the semiconductor sector (28.2%), more than offsetting declines in electronics (-7%) and autos (-1.4%). We believe external demand for AI chips remains strong, but supply chain disruptions and rising input costs may slow activity if the Iran war continues.

Chip output increased as manufacturers focused on high-end chips

Middle East conflict affected activity in March, making the outlook uncertain

Naphtha shortages are a major concern for the petrochemical sector for now. But second-round impacts will likely affect more manufacturing sectors. The government restricted naphtha exports starting March 27, but this merely balances import shortages.

Markets estimate that naphtha exports account for about 10% of total supply. Their role as feedstock for petrochemicals could significantly disrupt manufacturing supply chains.

Although alternative supply sources may help, the manufacturing sector could still face higher costs. Our 2Q26 GDP forecast has been revised downward since the war began. Downside risks have increased for the second quarter and beyond.

Investment also rose firmly in February

Facility investment gained 13.5% in February, with transportation equipment (40.4%) and machinery (3.8%) up. Construction investment also rose 19.5%, with both building (17.1%) and civil engineering (25.7%) rising sharply. Forward-looking orders data showed improvement. In a three-month comparison, machinery and construction orders rose by 2.6% and 17.4%, respectively.

March exports will continue to outperform amid strong chip demand

We expect exports to rise quite sharply by 47% in March. The first 20 days' export outcome showed chip exports surging 164%. We believe global oil supply challenges have not affected chip exports to date. However, rising commodity prices have led to a faster increase in imports (15%). Faster export growth should widen the trade surplus in March.

Given today’s strong February data and a solid March export outlook, we maintain our 0.6% quarter-over-quarter GDP growth forecast for 1Q26. However, we recognise that downside risks for 2Q26 GDP are increasing.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: The Return Of The Dividend King
data.description:
JNJ Stock: Pipeline Updates

I last covered Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) stock on Feb 19, 2026. That article was triggered by the FQ4 earnings report (ER) and also its then-new dividend declaration. The article rated the stock as a buy. Since that writing, a few notable changes have emerged and call for a reexamination. The rest of this article will focus on the changes of its new/pipeline drugs and also the valuation changes amid the market volatility since my last writing. After examining these changes, my conclusion is twofold: A) I see an enhanced growth outlook ahead, and B) the growth outlook is also discounted with the recent valuation correction. As such, I keep seeing a skewed return/risk ratio and reiterate my buy rating.

Let me start with the updates the company has announced to its new drugs and also pipelines since my last writing. The most notable development on my radar was the approval of its Icotyde (aka Ico or oral IL23) for psoriasis in mid-March. The Icotyde is expected to generate up to $8 bn in annual sales (see more details per the Guggenheim report quoted below):

Guggenheim reiterates Johnson & Johnson stock rating on FDA approval. The FDA approved Icotyde, an oral IL-23 treatment also known as icotrokinra, for plaque psoriasis. The approval came approximately four months earlier than broader market expectations for a standard review timeline. Guggenheim estimates Icotyde could generate more than $8 billion in annual peak sales for Johnson & Johnson, with the majority coming from psoriasis treatment.

JNJ’s annual sales have been slightly below $100 billion in 2025. As such, the Icotyde franchise alone could add about 8% annual growth to the company, as detailed below.

JNJ Stock: Growth Outlook

To better contextualize things, the chart below shows the stock’s dividend growth in the past. Given JNJ’s status as a dividend king (with 63 years of consecutive dividend increases), I feel its dividend growth rates are the best indicator of its true earnings growth. As seen, the stock has been facing growth pressure lately. In the past 10 years, the company has grown its payouts at a CAGR of around 5.65%, as seen. But the growth rates have slowed down in the past 3 and 5 years to about 5.18% and 4.78%, respectively, both below its own 10-year average and also the sector median.

With the new catalysts mentioned earlier, the EPS for Johnson & Johnson is projected to demonstrate an accelerated growth curve through the end of the decade, as shown by the latest consensus estimates below. Overall, the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) is about 8.9% between FY 2026 and 2030. Analysts estimate that its EPS will rise to $11.54 in FY 2026 and then to $16.27 by FY 2030. As earnings climb, the forward price-to-earnings ratio is expected to compress significantly, dropping from an FY1 P/E of 20.83x to only about 14.78x in 2030, illustrating an attractive combination of valuation and growth.

JNJ Stock: Valuation

To better illustrate the attractiveness, I will next apply a discounted dividend model (“DDM”) to assess JNJ’s fair valuation given its status as a dividend king. The particular DDM model I used is a two-stage model detailed in an earlier article. As such, I will only quote the end results to stay on point:

There are a total of 3 variables in this DDM: the discount rate, the growth rate in stage 1, and the terminal growth rate. For the discount rate, I relied on the so-called WACC, the weighted average cost of the capital model. The discount rate for JNJ is about 9.27% on average in recent years following this model.

For the growth rate in stage 1, I used the 8.9% EPS growth rate projected by consensus above, assuming its dividend will grow in tandem with EPS. For the terminal growth rate in stage 2, I plugged in 7.01% based on its following ROCE and reinvestment rates:

Long term growth rate is governed by the return on capital employed (“ROCE”) and the reinvestment rate (“RR”). My estimated ROCE for JNJ is around 68.7% as seen in the chart below, and my estimated RR is around 10.2% on average in recent years. With these inputs, JNJ’s terminal growth rate would be ~7.01% (68.7% ROCE x 10.2% RR = 7.01%).

With these rates and also its current annual dividend payout of $5.2, the DDM model finally shows a fair valuation for ~$273, suggesting a sizable margin of safety compared to its current share price of around $243.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Besides the Icotyde franchise, JNJ has also made progress on other pipeline products lately. For example, in early March, it also announced the achievement of a primary safety endpoint in a Phase 1 trial for a drug delivery system designed to administer its bladder cancer therapy erdafitinib. The positive results on this front could also better support its ongoing Phase 2 and Phase 3 studies designed to test Erda-iDRS. In terms of downside risks, Icotyde could face competition with other products such as ABBV’s Skyrizi and other orals (such as Otezla). The following research from BNP Paribas Equity Research analyst Navann Ty provides more details of the competition dynamics in this space:

However, Ty notes that AbbVie (ABBV) remains well-positioned in plaque psoriasis given Skyrizi’s strong efficacy and its quarterly injectable, which, after initiation doses, offers compliance benefits compared to once-daily oral dosing. “Both companies have indicated the entry of an oral could help to expand the market, providing more access and increasing penetration from the current 30-40%, implying Skyrizi should still have room to grow, in our view,” the analyst wrote.

In terms of downside risks, JNJ’s raw P/E ratios could seem stretched either by vertical or horizontal comparisons. As seen in the chart below, the Non-GAAP P/E (TTM) stands at 22.26x, which represents a 37% premium over the sector median of 16.26 and a 32% premium over its own five-year average of 16.89. However, I feel such a simple P/E ratio comparison to be out of context. I believe JNJ’s average P/E in the past few years is not representative. It is biased towards the lower end of its historical spectrum given the spinoff the company has gone through and also the macroscopic uncertainties surrounding drug pricing. These uncertainties are largely dissipated by now, in my view. And with the potential of growth acceleration as argued above, a comparison of its current P/E relative to the average in the past 5 years can be misleading.

To conclude, this JNJ reexamination is largely motivated by two recent changes surrounding the stock: the development with its new/pipeline drugs and also the valuation correction amid the broader SP500 market volatility. These changes have led me to see good odds for A) an acceleration of future profit growth and B) a misprice of this potential at the current share prices. Due to these considerations, I remain bullish and reiterate my buy rating.

data.title: Dana Incorporated: Positive Read-Throughs From Recent Investor Event
data.description:
I've retained a "Buy" rating for Dana Incorporated (DAN).

The company's promising financial prospects aren't fully factored into its valuations. Its new 2030 EPS target of $8 implies an annualized increase at the low-30s percentage level. But the stock is valued by the market at an undemanding low-teens P/E.

My earlier January 16, 2026, article touched on DAN's non-core disposal and its efforts to become leaner.

Anticipating Robust Earnings Growth in Coming Years

The firm hosted a "Capital Markets Day/CMD" last week. It underlined the commitment to previously set long-term aspirations outlined below.

Quantitative Roadmap

At the event, DAN also revealed a new FY30 bottom-line goal of $8.00/share. The midpoint of its FY26 guide was $2.50. I'm impressed by the implied +33.8% forward CAGR.

Management's expectations are realistic in my opinion, as I will explain in the subsequent sections.

Further Market Penetration and Portfolio Expansion Should Drive Top Line Acceleration

DAN aims to grab a bigger slice of the pie in "Commercial Vehicles/CVs" and "Light Vehicles/LVs."

The group sees itself outpacing the North American CV industry by +600bps on an annualized basis for the next five years. I like how it's changing the channel marketing strategy to make this happen.

In the CMD slides (source: S&P Capital IQ), it acknowledged that the CV-related operations have "been OEM-focused historically." Going forward, the priority is to "increase sales team size to develop deeper relationships" with over 2,000 dealers.

According to the latest investor event, DAN observed that clients are tweaking their mix of LVs with a "pullback from EV" to focus on "ICE programs." The company emphasized that it has ample "installed ICE capacity" without the need for "another large engineering investment cycle."

You would assume that automakers will turn to suppliers like Dana, who are ready to meet LV-related demand shifts swiftly.

Adjacent Markets With Good Potential

Separately, DAN is exploring fresh opportunities as detailed above.

Management indicated at the CMD that it has identified certain new solutions that are "mature, kind of low-risk applications of (Dana's) existing technology." The firm projected that it can generate $0.4 billion of additional sales between 2026 and 2030 from the entry into defense/powersport segments.

DAN's $10 billion turnover target translates into an FY25-30 CAGR of +5.9%. That's much better than the 2020-2025 annualized increase of +1.1%. I believe this is achievable for the reasons mentioned in this section.

Multiple Levers to Realize Margin Enhancement

The enterprise is targeting to widen its EBITDA-to-revenue by +4ppts during the 2026-2030 period.

I'm positive on DAN's profitability discipline. Looking ahead, it's insisting on a "minimum 10% EBITDA margin" for every single "product line and SKU" based on its CMD disclosures.

The group's internal checks found that 500+ SKUs were sold at a loss. I can imagine Dana either raising the prices or discontinuing these products to address this issue. The potential financial upside is huge, given that the remaining 100+ parts boasted an average low-20s EBITDA-to-top line.

Also, you would think it makes sense for DAN to place a greater emphasis on divisions with superior financial profiles.

The company estimates that the "incremental margin" for its "Aftermarket" unit is about 33%. At the late-March Investor Day, it shared that "we've been aggressively working to break into this (retail) channel" by working closer with "major big-box retailers." This gives me confidence that the sales contribution from this high-return business will rise over time.

In my view, there are also laggards that should catch up over time thanks to scale economies. DAN's EV operations boasted a modest EBITDA-to-sales of 4% last year. The firm expects this metric to go above 10% by '30, when EV-related revenue hits the $1 billion mark.

I see increased automation playing a key role in Dana's profit optimization as well. It intends to quadruple the number of "Autonomous Mobile Robots" or "AMRs" deployed in its factories to 200 within the next few years.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

DAN's actual top-line growth might disappoint investors in the event of a prolonged global economic slowdown. It's natural that discretionary vehicle purchases will be deferred in challenging times.

The enterprise may not attain its margin targets, assuming a failure to execute well on major profitability-related initiatives. A bear-case scenario will have Dana sticking to unprofitable SKUs and growing its aftermarket division slowly.

My Closing Thoughts

I continue to favor this name as an investment candidate.

The stock is undervalued based on the "price-to-earnings growth" or "PEG" ratio.

DAN currently trades at a PEG of 0.36 times. That's way below the 1x indicative of fair valuation. I've arrived at this figure by considering its forward P/E of 12.3x and the 33.8% FY2026-2030 CAGR cited above.

My take is that the ongoing progress towards its '30 financial targets will help to re-rate its shares.
